Description: The student government passed a resolution to condemn Hamas in a vote of 16 yes, 4 no, 1 abstention. The resolution defined Hamas as a "genocidal terrorist organization" and called for the swift and complete dismantling of Hamas for the sake of humanity and eventual peace in the region.

A bill, titled “A Resolution to Condemn Hamas and Antisemitism at UC Santa Barbara,” passed with 75% voting in favor. The bill called on Associated Students (A.S.) to “unequivocally condemn Hamas” and “mourn all civilian loss of life in Israel and Gaza as a result of the ensuing war,” as well as “denounce the hateful rhetoric of pro-Hamas student groups as antisemitic, reprehensible, and morally despicable.”

The student senate passed a resolution urging the university to proclaim April 29 as #EndJewHatred Day in a 16 in favor, one against and eight abstentions vote. According to the Jewish Journal, "The resolution stated that one of the reasons the day is necessary is the various incidents that happened on campus in January, citing 'the vandalization of a classroom with false allegations that the State of Israel, the only Jewish State, a perpetrator of genocide, femicide, apartheid, ethnic cleansing and white supremacy, as well as the dissemination of flyers with heinously antisemitic propaganda and tropes impacting the emotional and mental well-being of Jewish students throughout the community.' The resolution also noted that the student senate held a meeting on Yom Kippur in 2022, thus 'senators, public forum speakers and spectators were unable to participate due to religious observance.'"

The Student Senate of University of Kansas passed a resolution condemning antisemitism in a 38-0 vote, with two senators abstaining, the resolution also called on administrators to "sustain and support" the university’s Jewish Studies program.

The student government passed a resolution calling for the adoption of the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance (IHRA) working definition of Antisemitism with 15 for, 1 against and 6 abstentions.
The BU student government passed a resolution with 33 votes that called on BU to adopt the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance’s definition of antisemitism.

UCSB Associated Students unanimously passed "A Resolution in Support of the Jewish Community and the Condemnation of Antisemitism" which, according to the Daily Nexis, "condemned the recent acts of antisemitism and called on the Office of the Vice Chancellor for Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion to 'formulate a response that will ensure the safety and security of Jewish students on campus.' It also asked the University of California Student Association to meet with representatives from the Jewish community to 'better develop a systemwide response to Jew hatred on our campuses.'"

The University of Houston’s Student Government Association unanimously passed the Religious Support Bill, which confirms each student’s right to worship freely, condemns harassment based on religious affiliation, acknowledges that such harassment continues to be an issue at the University of Houston, expresses condolences to those who experience violence based on their religious community, calls on students and administrators to cultivate a more welcoming campus environment, and prohibits future SGA legislation from singling out any religious group. The final clause states, "Be it further resolved, that for future transparency, SGA legislation shall not single out any religious group, and shall address religious groups collectively."

In a special meeting, the Undergraduate Student Government (USG) passed a resolution to "condemn and combat antisemitism" which calls on the Office of the Dean of Undergraduate Students (ODUS) and the Office of the Dean of Graduate Students to increase trainings on antisemitism, including "trainings for student leaders, bias trainings for students, and annual orientation programming." The preamble noted that "the University hosts trainings addressing antisemitism for students, faculty, staff, and administrators, and those trainings use the [IHRA] definition of antisemitism with its examples."

The student government passed a statement denouncing antisemitism that expressed, "On our campus, Jewish students, faculty, staff, and community members repeatedly convey mistreatment, including their fear of being attacked or targeted for speaking out in their own defense against hate and antisemitism. All students, faculty, and staff should be able to speak, teach, or publish without fear of retribution."
The Student Senate passed a resolution to condemn antisemitic attacks in the United States and "commits to supporting students of the Jewish faith" on campus.
ASUC Senate approved a bill to condemn antisemitism while nine of 20 senators chose to be absent in protest of the resolution.

The Student Government unanimously passed a resolution that urged the administration to ban all music by Kanye West in solidarity with Jewish students at the University after Kanye West made a series of antisemitic and anti-Zionist remarks online.
Cornell University Student Assembly passed a resolution titled "Resolution Five: Condemning Antisemitism".

The University of Wisconsin-Madison’s student government unanimously passed a resolution condemning antisemitism titled "Resolution Addressing Anti-Semitism on Campus," which urged the university administration to "discipline the perpetrators of these [antisemitic] acts, if and when known, and the perpetrators of all future antisemitic and racist acts."
The student government passed a resolution that called for the adoption of the IHRA definition of antisemitism.

The student government board voted to support the IHRA definition, which follows on the heels of the university formally adopting the IHRA definition on March 30, 2022. The IHRA definition will now be included in the university’s nondiscriminatory policy.

According to IDS News, the IUSG Congress "passed a non-binding internal resolution condemning recent online antisemitic comments directed toward Jewish students at IU. IUSG intends to adopt the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance’s non-legally binding working definition of antisemitism and encourages Indiana University to do the same. As the first non-binding internal resolution of Congress, the resolution holds no official weight as an IUSG policy, according to the press release."
A resolution endorsing the IHRA definition of antisemitism passed unanimously in the student government.

The student government passed a "Statement to Condemn Antisemitism" that expressed, "De Anza Student Government Senate is firmly against antisemitism, defined as prejudice and discrimination against Jews and those perceived as Jews. The DASG Senate recognizes that existing organizations and definitions each have their controversies and limitations attached to them. As a result, we will not be officially endorsing any existing definitions or organizations. We condemn the antisemitism that has occurred on De Anza’s campus, particularly the putting up of antisemitic posters in 2017 that denied the occurrence of the Holocaust. The DASG Senate and De Anza College do not tolerate discrimination, and any instances of antisemitism on campus should be reported to Judicial Affairs to be dealt with appropriately by the college. The fight against antisemitism is a part of a larger fight against white supremacy, and the DASG Senate is committed to challenging all forms of bigotry and discrimination."
The student government reportedly passed a resolution supporting the IHRA definition of antisemitism. Entry to be updated when more information is available.
The student government unanimously passed a resolution supporting the IHRA definition of antisemitism.
In an executive order, the undergraduate student government adopted the IHRA definition of antisemitism.
A student government resolution adopting the IHRA definition of antisemitism passed unanimously by the review board and then the executive board.

The Student Senate unanimously passed Resolution SS 2021-37, "A Resolution Acknowledging, Defining and Condemning Antisemitism in Solidarity with Jewish Students." The resolution calls for the University to adopt the IHRA definition of antisemitism.

The student government passed resolution SSB 17, which utilized the IHRA definition of antisemitism and called for the creation of a Jewish Constituency Senator position, alongside the numerous other ethnic constituency senator positions (after initially denying this position in part because of the position's affiliation with Hillel - see AMCHA tracker).
A campus-wide referendum to adopt the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance (IHRA) working definition of antisemitism passed with 58% of the vote.

The student government unanimously passed a bill condemning antisemitism and adopting the IHRA definition. The bill includes “actionable steps” for SA members and other community leaders to access anti-Semitism training, including a Stop BIAS program through Syracuse Hillel and encourages the SA to propose the definition to SU’s administration.

The student government passed a resolution adopting the IHRA definition of antisemitism. MSU Jewish students later detailed harassment following the passage of this resolution, which subsequently resulted in them feeling forced into "rescind[ing] the original bill."
The student government unanimously passed a resolution in support of adopting the IHRA definition of antisemitism.

The student government of The University of Texas at Austin (UT Austin) unanimously passed Resolution A.R. 09, which calls on the UT Austin student government to: commit to better address anti-Jewish sentiments on campus; adopt the IHRA definition for anti-Semitism; and issue “a pledge of support for better Jewish inclusion and protection.”
UC San Diego’s Associated Students (on 2/24) and prior to that, the Graduate Students Association (on 2/22) both unanimously passed resolutions adopting the IHRA definition of antisemitism.
The student government unanimously passed a resolution adopting the IHRA definition of antisemitism.

The student government unanimously passed "A Resolution Condemning Antisemitism and Calling for Institutional Representation for Jewish Students" that stated, "The purpose of this resolution is to urge the University of Georgia to adopt the official definition of Antisemitism as stated by the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance." In 2015, the student government had passed a resolution expanding study abroad options in Israel.
The student government unanimously passed a resolution adopting the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance (IHRA) definition of antisemitism.

The student government passed a resolution against antisemitism that included adoption of the IHRA definition in a 47 yes, 0 no and 27 abstentions vote. The resolution stated, "Both the SGA and Northeastern University formally adopt the IHRA definition and all examples included within the definition of antisemitism as a guideline in order to effectively counter and act upon any future antisemitic incidents that may occur on campus."
The CCNY USG passed a resolution against antisemitism that included adoption of the IHRA definition of antisemitism.
The student senate adopted the IHRA definition of antisemitism.
A resolution calling on the University administration to adopt the IHRA definition of antisemitism passed unanimously in the student government.
The student government passed a resolution against antisemitism that included the adoption of the IHRA definition of antisemitism.

In a 28-0 vote, the student government unanimously passed a resolution against antisemitism that calls on the College to adopt the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance (IHRA) definition of antisemitism.

The Student Government Association passed a controversial and watered-down resolution 2021-026, that had been introduced by SJP members to counter an original resolution proposing the IHRA definition drafted by Jewish students, 2021-021. Due to the IHRA's definition's inclusion in the original resolution, backlash ensued by SJP and others, including the filing of numerous bias reports to the Office of Student Affairs, which resulted in the University's involvement to cause an injunction and the resolution being struck from the Senate floor. The resolution that passed in the end, 2021-026, generically denounced antisemitism and stated that SGA will not address the definition of antisemitism at all again this term, to the objection of numerous Jewish students.

Senate Resolution 5, which condemns antisemitism, was passed by a vote of 15-1-3. The resolution called on the ASU President to release a public statement the recent antisemitic incidents on campus, on ASU to adopt the IHRA definition of antisemitism and on the Office of Educational Outreach and Student Services to establish a Jewish Cultural Coalition. Seven Jewish organizations on campus co-signed the resolution, including Chabad at ASU, the Hillel at ASU Student Leadership Board, Jewish Arizonans on Campus, Students Supporting Israel at ASU, Sun Devil Public Affairs Committee, Alpha Epsilon Pi Fraternity and Alpha Epsilon Phi Sorority.

Resolution 59, which acknowledges the IHRA definition of antisemitism and calls for increased representation of Jewish students by the Florida State University (FSU) Student Senate, passed on July 15, 2020 with a vote of 26 in favor, 14 against, and 0 abstentions after previously being filibustered. The student senate had previously tabled Resolution 59 on July 9, 2020 after not voting on the resolution after a five-hour meeting in which it was filibustered. It had first passed readily in the Student Life & Academic Affairs Committee before that and moved onto the Student Senate for a vote. However, following a campaign by SJP for supporters to personally target and email senators in objection of the resolution, with a template email that utilized antisemitic language that demonized Israel, it was then filibustered. According to SJP, the resolution was tabled because, "Senators suggested that our emails and public comments are shaking their belief in a pro-Israel consensus on campus and need to do more consultation with constituents before voting".

In February 2021, an anti-IHRA resolution introduced by a controversial former student senate president for his past antisemitic statements, which called on FSU to “rescind” its adoption of IHRA, failed in committee before it could reach the senate floor

The board members of Associated Students (AS) unanimously approved and sponsored the “Expressing Solidarity for Jewish Students at Santa Monica College” resolution at a Zoom-hosted board meeting. The passed resolution states, "Jewish undergraduate students are subjected to discrimination and harassment" as well as commendably expresses, "the A.S. finds it unacceptable to harass, intimidate, marginalize, exclude or demonize any member of our community college on the basis of their identity". The resolution resolves that, "the A.S. advocates for SMC’s commitment to publicly denounce any incidents of antisemitism that occur and affect the SMC community". During the course of the discussion, the IHRA definition of antisemitism was removed.

Following the introduction of a BDS resolution and discussions on campus, the Undergraduate Student Government Tempe passed Resolution 12, "A Resolution to Stand With Jewish Students at ASU" that stated, "NOTING that ASU students who are part of the Jewish community have expressed concerns over their own safety on-campus to the administration and police force in light of recent events, specifically Nazi propaganda... BE IT RESOLVED that the Undergraduate Student Government of Tempe supports all students in mutual civil dialogue and debate in an environment that is free from threat and intimidation; BE IT FURTHER RESOLVED that the Undergraduate Student Government of Tempe does not support any type of intimidation, hate speech, or threats directed towards anyone, does not support anti-Semitism, and stands by the Jewish community..."

The Student Association Senate passed a resolution titled, "The Anti-Semitism Condemnation Act,” that approved some aspects of the IHRA Definition and State Department definitions of antisemitism, but removed additional clauses that claimed the state of Israel is a ‘racist endeavor’ and that Israel has the right to exist as a Jewish state before being passed. The resolution states that, "Some Jewish students at The George Washington University have expressed that anti-Zionism can be a mask for anti-Semitism, and Jewish communities are often targets for anti-Israel animus" and endorsed the notion that antisemitism includes statements equating Israel to the Nazis, that accuses Jews of dual loyalty to Israel or applies double standards to Israel, as we as that “denying the Jewish people the right to self-determination" is antisemitic.
Category: General A/S, SD Definition, IHRA Definition

Description: Following a swastika incident on campus, the Associated Students of Colorado State University unanimously voted to pass a resolution titled, "Resolution 4904 — Standing united against campus antisemitism" in support of the campus Jewish community as well as a bill, titled, "Bill 4905 — Approval of BSOF funding for Holocaust Awareness Week" which passed with a unanimous roll call vote.

The UC Davis Student Government ruled that a 2015 BDS resolution was unconstitutional, stating in the ruling: "The passing of this [BDS] Resolution has led to the discrimination and harassment of students whose ethnicity, national origin or political beliefs are in opposition to the content of the Resolution. As discussed during the [May 8, 2019] hearing, the verbiage within the Resolution has caused harassment against many students.” The ruling found that the BDS resolution contravened a section of the ASUCD Constitution that commits the Senate to “promote the welfare and interests of the members of the ASUCD, explaining that "while this [BDS} Resolution caters to the welfare of a group of students, it does so at the expense of the welfare of other students.” The BDS resolution was also found to be in breach of the ASUCD Student Bill of Rights, which rejects “discrimination and harassment on the basis of … ethnicity … national origin … or political belief.”

Associated Students, Inc. (ASI) passed the Freedom of Speech and Anti-Discrimination bylaw amendment. According to Mustang News, "Section 4.7.1.3 of the bill states that “neither the board nor any body or organization under its jurisdiction … shall not practice or sanction any unlawful discriminatory practice related to: veteran status, uniformed service member status, race, color, religion, gender identity, gender expression, sex, sexual orientation, pregnancy (including childbirth, lactation, or related medical conditions), age, national origin or ancestry, immigration status, citizenship status, physical or mental disability, or genetic information (including testing and characteristics).” According to two of the bill's drafters the bill would help, "Jewish students who are concerned about the rise of antisemitism and antizionism nationally and especially on college campuses, in addition to other forms of xenophobia and discrimination of marginalized communities at Cal Poly SLO... [BDS] would be categorized as a form of discrimination [which would be] protect[ed] against with this bill... [The hope is that if BDS ever comes to Cal Poly, that] future leaders [will] look at this piece of legislation and thoroughly examine whether passing BDS would go against the bylaws of our organization and pose a risk to Associated Students Inc.”

A resolution was passed in the student government that stated, "Antisemitism includes the delegitimization of Israel through denying Jewish people their right to self-determination and denying Israel the right to exist. The University of Saint Thomas Undergraduate Government adopts the definition of antisemitism stated in the first section. The University of Saint Thomas Undergraduate Government will recognize that the Jewish people, like all peoples, have a collective right to self-determination."
Category: General A/S, SD Definition, IHRA Definition

Description: The Associated Student Government Senate passed, “A Resolution to Reaffirm Support for Jewish and Israeli Students at the University of Arkansas” in a 40-2 vote. The resolution was drafted in “support of Jewish and Israeli students” on campus and labels the BDS movement as anti-Semitic as well as “promotes the inclusion of Jewish and Israeli students and their beliefs and values on campus.”

A resolution entitled “Combating Anti-Semitism” was passed by the student government which was defined as “meaningless” by many members and leaders of the campus Jewish community after explicit requests of the Jewish community, including calling for the University to adopt the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance’s definition of antisemitism, were ignored and the resolution was substantially amended. One Jewish student leader expressed that the “ISG voted in opposition to the Jewish community. They passed an amended resolution, supposedly about anti-Semitism, which addressed a wide variety of other ‘isms’ or minority groups and misrepresented entirely what anti-Semitism is... The ISG emboldened anti-Semitism on this campus, silenced Jewish students and voted directly against the Jewish community by passing a resolution that it was told explicitly did not address anti-Semitism.”

A resolution against antisemitism passed unanimously in the undergraduate student senate. The resolution referenced the International Holocaust Remembrance Association (IHRA) definition of antisemitism.

The student government passed a resolution that defined antisemitism using the State Department definition of antisemitism, acknowledged that “some, but not all, criticisms of and attacks on the State of Israel can be anti-Semitic dog-whistles” and bound the student government to “not deliberately facilitate, promote, fund, or participate in any activities that directly promote anti-Semitism or undermine the rights of Jewish people.”
The student government passed a resolution that defined antisemitism using the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance (IHRA) definition. Date of passage is approximated.

The Senate passed the “Support of Aggie Israelis and Protection of Jewish Students Resolution” with a vote of 38 in favor, 6 against, and 12 abstentions. The resolution contained anti-BDS language, including stating, “According to the definition of anti-Semitism adopted by the U.S. Department of State, European Union, Canada, and major Jewish organizations, the Boycott, Divestment, and Sanctions (BDS) Movement is anti-Semitic in its effect, if not in its intent…Student Senate stands with Jewish and Israeli students as a valued member of the Aggie family; and While the Student Senate will respect the right of all students to freedom of speech, it will not facilitate, promote, or participate in any activities that promote BDS or any other form of anti-Semitism.”

The Associated Students of Madison unanimously passed a resolution against antisemitism that cites the U.S. State Department’s definition of antisemitism, and notes “anti-Semitic incidents tend to occur when anti-Israel legislation are introduced in student government or in a student body.”

The student government at Chapman University in California passed a resolution condemning anti-Semitism titled, “Resolution To Condemn Campus Anti-Semitism,” which was approved by a vote of 8-0-3. The resolution refers to “overwhelming incidents of anti-Semitism occurring nationwide, locally, and directly on campus,” and commits the student government to “an official stand against anti-Semitism” by adopting the U.S. State Department’s definition of anti-Semitism.

San Diego State University Associated Students (A.S.) passed “A Resolution to Condemn Anti-Semitism.” In passing this resolution, A.S. formally adopted the State Department definition of antisemitism, which includes demonization, double standards, and delegitimization of Israel. They recognized, “that the Jewish people, like all peoples, have a collective right to self-determination” and condemned attempts to “undermine” these rights.

The student government passed a resolution against antisemitism in a 18-0-3 vote. The resolution initially used the State Department definition of antisemitism as well as anti-BDS language. However, the final resolution passed removed the anti-BDS language as well as re-defined antisemitism as “hostility toward, or discrimination against, Jewish persons as a religious, ethnic, or racial group.”
The student government of The University of Texas at Austin (UT Austin) unanimously approved A.R. 26, which condemned recent antisemitic incidents in and around the campus.

The Student Government passed a resolution against antisemitism titled, “The Fight Against Anti-Semitism Resolution” that included the U.S. State Department definition of antisemitism. The resolution was brought to SGA’s attention due to instances of antisemitism within the ECU community including graffiti targeting Jewish students found on campus and in off campus homes of Jewish students.

The Stanford University student senate unanimously approved a resolution defining and condemning anti-Semitism. The resolution was contentious prior to passing as numerous statements (including the IHRA definition) were removed from it. Final wording stated that “many Jewish students worry that implicit anti-Jewish biases may give rise to disproportionate criticism of the State of Israel or Zionism as an ideology.” The measure also condemned “delegitimization of Israel based explicitly on the state’s Jewish nature” and recognized that “the collective rights to self-determination of the Jewish people are no different than any other people.”

Jewish Student Association and Students Supporting Israel presented an antisemitism resolution that unanimously passed in Student Government. The resolution states, “While the membership of student government will respect the right of all students to exercise their freedom of speech, as an organizational body it will not facilitate, promote, fund or participate in any activities that directly or indirectly promote anti-Semitism or undermine the rights of the Jewish people to self-determination.” The resolution also mentions the boycott, divestment and sanctions movement by noting, “The membership of Capital University’s student government recognizes that the Jewish people, like all peoples, have a collective right to self-determination, and considers attempts to undermine these rights to be a form of bigotry.

Students Supporting Israel at Indiana University passed a Resolution in Student Government that adopts State Dep’t definition of antisemitism relative to Israel. The resolution stated, “The Indiana University Student Association adopts the definition of anti-Semitism as stated above as well as the State Department’s understanding of Anti-Semitism relative to Israel… The Indiana University Student Association recognizes that the Jewish people, like all peoples, have a collective right to self-determination, and considers attempts to undermine these rights, including the global BDS Movement against Israel, to be a form of bigotry"

The Student Senate of Texas A&M University unanimously passed the country’s first pro-Israel student government resolution. The resolution, “A resolution supporting the nation of Israel from the Student Senate of Texas A&M University,” called upon the university to invest in Israel and to expand study-abroad opportunities for A&M students in Israel.”

The Associated Students Senate of the University of Nevada, Reno unanimously passes a Resolution for Semitic Tolerance which states: "Whereas, anti-Semitism is a growing trend on college campuses; Whereas, anti-Semitic actions are correlated with movements to boycott, divest, and sanction against Israel"
The Associated Students Senate passes a resolution to condemn antisemitism with a vote of 23-0 with one abstention. The resolution alludes to the US State Department's definition of antisemitism.

The undergraduate student government unanimously passed “A Resolution Condemning Anti-Semitism” which included the full US State Department's definition of antisemitism, including demonizing or delegitimizing Isreal, and holding Israel to a double standard.

The undergraduate student government unanimously passed “A Bill Condemning Anti-Semitism” which alluded to the US State Department's definition of antisemitism and created an ad-hoc committee to discuss and educate about antisemitism on campus.
The Student Government Association passed a resolution that encouraged the expansion of study abroad programs in Israel. Exact date of passage is unknown.

According to The Algemeiner, the Associated Students of Western Washington University (ASWWU) became the first North American university student government to pass a proactive resolution against the Boycott, Divestment and Sanctions (BDS) movement. In a 7-0 vote, ASWWU resolved that it would not consider BDS resolutions based on national origin because of the “tensions between students related to foreign conflicts should be managed in a healthy and collaborative manner rather than be exacerbated.” Additionally, the resolution noted that BDS campaigns could result in students being targeted based on their nationality and lead to “disrespectful bias, hostility, hate, or harassment.”

The Minnesota Student Association passed a resolution that supported “expanding study abroad opportunities in the Middle East, specifically Israel, with a concentration in business, science, engineering, and agriculture.”
